Your understanding is right. This is a tricky scenario: if you need authentication on a more finely-grained level than hostname, you'll need to override urllib3's connection pooling logic to understand that. That's a tricky thing, but do-able.

The slightly easier thing to do would be to use one session per authentication identity, and then the code in #51. That way you can hang the authentication off the session and ensure that you never get the wrong one, at the cost of slightly reduced connection pooling efficiency.

psq is pretty cool, but I believe it's a bit specialized into the niche of low configuration task queue. For example, I was under the impression that it only worked for topics that began with the name "psq-" and assumed the existence of a shared subscription on that queue.

Supporting callbacks on some arbitrary subscription would either be a feature request on gcloud-pubsub for callbacks or a feature request on psq for supporting a single, arbitrary subscription. I could file the latter, if you'd prefer not to add callbacks on this side, but I think I'd prefer it be added to gcloud-pubsub. It seems like a thread that continually polled for messages and dispatched them to a handler would be pretty much universally useful to gcloud-pubsub users.

@rmmh The reason that `insert_data` takes tuples is that most "row oriented" Python modules (the DB-API, the `csv` stdlib module) deal with rows as tuples, not as mappings.  We also work to consume / return the values as the correct native Python types, according to the schema type, which is *not* the same as the on-the-wire JSON values (e.g., datetimes, bools, bytes, etc.)

I think this is a result of a defect in the library `future` that we've used to bridge the gap between Python 2 and Python 3.

In `stacker/actions/build.py`, we have used `str` to force all the parameters into a string so it could be accepted by Cloudformation. On seeing the use of `str`, `future` tries to replace that with a class defined in `future.types.newstr`, so it behaves more or less like unicode(thus bridging the gap).

The issue is the `newstr` instance is later transformed into a `newbytes` instance, and it cannot be quoted because of a flaw in `future`. The following snippet will reproduce the error above:

```
#!/usr/bin/env python
from builtins import bytes         # This comes from `future`
from urllib import quote

quote(bytes('10.128.0.0/16'), safe='')
```

FYI, that 49 comes from the ascii repr of '1'.

There are several ways to fix this issue:

1. report this flaw to `future` and ask them to fix this from upstream.

This should be our best approach, however, I think the development of `future` is somehow stagnant, so it could take a very long time.

2. Avoid the use of `newstr`/`newbytes`.

It is not possible to avoid it altogether, but I do think we could avoid the use of `newstr`/`newbytes` in this specific case, since we are not really looking for that unicode behaviour.

I'll push a commit to my branch and fix this.

> If it's a daemon, it can also route to only two queues, one for email and one for IRC, that two other daemons would consume, and not one queue per user as with the header exchange, that those daemons would all have to subscribe to (and add new subscriptions when new users are registered, etc).

One motivation for the one-queue-per-user approach is it makes batching messages into digests trivial. Otherwise you have to do what FMN is doing now, which is dropping messages that are destined for people who want batching into a separate queue (or the database, in FMN's case). With the queue per user approach, everything is the same, it's just some queues have consumers all the time, and some have a Twisted call loop at an interval applied to them in the sending daemon.

Another reason I thought the one-queue-per-user approach would be good is that RabbitMQ's job is to quickly route messages and hold them in queues, so if we constrained ourselves to things it can route, notifications will be very fast.

It's definitely not a clear-cut situation. As you pointed out, you need something to keep the bindings up-to-date. To me, though, that feels like an easier task than doing the routing and batch queuing ourselves. And if we're not going to offer a batching feature (although I think it is fairly widely used), things get easier with the 2 queue based approach.

I'd really like to make a PoC of my plan before seriously pitching it, though. Whatever direction we go, I think we can safely add the metadata in the library populated from standard accessors from https://github.com/fedora-infra/fedora-messaging/issues/28.

I've not been able to reproduce this behavior, but just had a forehead-slapping moment when re-examining the code you sent over earlier:

```
http_proxy=googleads.common.ProxyConfig.Proxy('proxy host w/o http://', 8080)
https_proxy=googleads.common.ProxyConfig.Proxy('proxy host w/o https://', 8080)
proxy_config = googleads.common.ProxyConfig(http_proxy=http_proxy, https_proxy=https_proxy)
client = adwords.AdWordsClient(DEVELOPER_TOKEN, OAUTH_CLIENT, USER_AGENT, client_customer_id=account_id, proxy_config=proxy_config)
```
It's actually not enough to provide the ProxyConfig instance to the AdWordsClient. In order for it to be applied to whichever `GoogleOAuth2Client` subclass you're using for OAuth 2.0, you need to instantiate it with a `proxy_config` keyword argument. It looks like you passed the ProxyConfig over to the AdWordsClient, but forgot to pass it to the GoogleRefreshTokenClient(?) you were using.

@yashu-seth I was thinking about how the fallback option would work. So, let's say someone implements a new Distribution class and implements just the marginalize method (which has an efficient implementation and just needs to modify the parameters of the distribution). Now, let's say he calls the product method in which case our implementation would fall back to the default product implementation and now our returned distribution would not be the same distribution. So, now if he calls the marginalize method after this, it would fail.

So, for dealing with situations like this I think we will need to make all the methods (which might change the distribution) abstract. Or we can change the object type to `CustomDistribution` whenever we  fall back to the default implementation. What do you think ?

> there's more than one way of converting that data

Yes, exactly that’s my concern. We won’t get streamlined workflows when the same piece of data can be converted to output in different ways. (Unless there’s a specification to clearly decide which way is correct).

But thanks for your patient explanation. It sounds like you’d like to have something in the exact format of `kerning.plist`, but without the associated semantics. Have you considered putting your files into the [data directory](http://unifiedfontobject.org/versions/ufo3/data/) inside the UFO, for example as `data/raw-kerning.plist` or so?

If that wasn’t an option for some reason (curious to hear more), what would you think of defining a custom property in [lib.plist](http://unifiedfontobject.org/versions/ufo3/lib.plist/) that instructs the compiler to ignore `kerning.plist` etc.?

Basically, I’m looking for a way to making builds “reproducible”, meaning it should be clearly defined how to convert some piece of input to output. If we add custom compiler options that aren’t part of the UFO file itself, the build process becomes harder to reproduce because it’s difficult to know when to pass what flag.

So from what I can see, `include_package_data=True` **only** works if the "data" is "inside a package". The only mention of licensing information in a bdist or wheel comes in the metadata which just says the kind of license (e.g. `"MIT License"`).

@zhaohanweng-- not a bad idea.  Moving it to the "default_server" vhost that's listening on 9090 would be good because that vhost never uses PROXY protocol-- which would be important if you were looking to hit that endpoint from _within_ the cluster.

If a user opts into exposing the endpoint outside the cluster, then I believe this location block should appear on the "default_server" for _both_ ports 80 and 9090.

Further, there's never any guarantee that port 9090 isn't exposed to the outside world.  It _can_ be, depending on what modifications a user might make to their load balancer.  As such, it _might_ be good to actually lock that location block down to access only by `127.0.0.1` using the `allow` directive-- at least in the case where a user has not opted into exposing it.  (Tangent: we've done something similar in v2.  We never expose it to the outside world, but we make it available only from _within_ the same pod since we run an agent that hits that endpoint to collect these metrics.)

I like this idea, but I think it gets triaged pretty low since we are in full swing on v2 development right now.  I'm going to mark this issue as "help wanted," so if you might like to take a crack at this, please feel free.

@mingwandroid `deactivate` almost completely reverses the original activation. There are a few caveats that may cause slight reordering of the `PATH` (but only the conda related paths are reordered, nothing else from the user's `PATH` is touched). Now, unlike previous versions of conda, if the user modifies their `PATH` while in an environment and then calls `deactivate` only the conda environment on the `PATH` is removed/updated, the other non-conda modified parts remain as the user changed.

Of course they are lesser shells, I really do not expect anyone to work in `dash` (as an aside there are special places for those who use spaces in pathnames and also work in `csh`/`tcsh`). What I do see is that each time I was able to incorporate a new shell I found that I was able to create a more robust solution. Besides it's cool to call Conda OS **and** shell-agnostic :)

It looks like you have quotes in some PATH entry that should not be there, or some script has them done inappropriately.  Can you tell us more about what Anaconda installation you started from?  What steps have you tried to update conda and/or anaconda?  Did any of those steps fail?

Without requests, I'm afraid your installation might be corrupt.  You might need to reinstall Anaconda/Miniconda.

@johanneskoester, thanks for your potential offer of help!  Tell me more about "pinning beyond versions" - the point of https://github.com/conda/conda-build/issues/1142 is to try to figure out how to encode all of that information, and how to make each facet configurable.  Hashes are good fingerprints, but I'm not sure of their utility in actually selecting a package.  HashDist is an interesting model, but quite a bit less flexible than what we have now - you only get exact matches.

For the Bioconda hackathon, I'd be thrilled to have any of your time in moving https://github.com/conda/conda-build/issues/1142 forward.  Please take a look at that issue.  Does it scope the problem well enough right now?  If not, what's missing?  I'm wide open to implementations.  I think that everyone involved with the history of that issue (yourself included) has given us interesting potential APIs.  I am partial to the config files proposed by @ukoethe and @stuarteberg (as opposed to solely command line flags), but open to evolution from there.

There are a few facets that I have in mind, especially regarding that conda-build PR:
1) need to develop a schema for what information gets encoded, and how.  This is especially true for non-version information, such as compiler flags.  I'm open to proposals, and have no solid ideas at the moment.
2) need an expression somehow for selecting configurations at install time, as well as at build time.  My hope was that one set of config files would serve both purposes - in other words, the same config files could be used for either purpose.  This was what I asked @kalefranz to look into with #3679.  These are "Core Dependency Trees" - CDTs.  If hashes are useful for keying builds, then also need to figure out how hashes can be derived from a CDT without actually building a package - or otherwise, we'll need a more complicated composite key of the many facets.  At install time, I think the only valid assumption is that the user will have a CDT, and not hashes based on built packages for what they want.
3) need a way to capture things that can't necessarily be installed, such as particular symbol versions or restrictively-licensed software.  Our idea right now is to make conda packages that check versions, rather than actually installing software.  This way, the dependencies can be explicit, but the actual behavior might be different from how it is now.
4) need a collection of critical system information to gather, and then the scripts or whatever to gather it. The cause of this issue is the typical one: `features`! See:
```
$ alias parse_info='python -c '"'"'import sys,json; print(*(" ".join(map(repr, (x["url"], x.get("features"), x.get("track_features")))) for x in sum(json.load(sys.stdin).values(), [])), sep="\n")'"'"
```

`openjdk-8.0.121-1.tar.bz2` is the last version without `track_features`.

In contrast to the usual `features`-related issues, this one is caused by a `feature`-providing package for which older versions without `track_features="openjdk"` exist.
So, the problem here is that
https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/f1fe43dfd918d7896b254b1465c7d77ae155ffb3/conda/resolve.py#L886-L889
has no information to distinguish between already installed `features` and newly introduced ones and thus also penalizes the installed `defaults::openjdk=8.0.152`. To fix this, we'd need to somehow provide that information to `conda.resolve.Resolve.generate_feature_count`.
I think this issue can be closed now, in lieu of the others that have been opened branching from here.

My opinion is: Having a few envs for certain specific purposes is fine. Those purposes are:

    1. To store only conda in order to simplify conda's code.
    2. To store the root/Administrator installed, read only packages that everyone can use.
    3. To store the user's customizations of that env.

Making this arbitrarily cascadable would be really confusing and difficult to manage and communicate. We'd also run out of PATH on Windows quite quickly.

Unfortunately, this cannot be easily coded around in any way I have been able to imagine.  The closest I have been able to come has been with the addition of the _tasks API in the most recent releases of Elasticsearch, but it amounts to a gamble and a guess.

The problem is that when you initiate a forceMerge in Elasticsearch, the base Lucene command offers no way to track the results, other than to count the number of segments per shard, which has proven an unreliable way to track status, especially if you accidentally have more data come in.  The only reliable way Elasticsearch allows you to tell if a forceMerge is done is to keep the client connection open until it has returned control to the client.  Should that client connection close while you are waiting—like with your Lambda—there is essentially no way to reconnect to that process without it being that guess and a gamble.  ForceMerges don’t even allow API-level blocking responses.  If one has been initiated and is in progress, you can still initiate another, but it will be opaquely blocked and put on hold until the current one has completed—and Elasticsearch won’t tell you that this is the case.  

The forceMerge API is very ripe for changes that correct these issues, but I don’t know if/when they will ever happen.  As stated, you _can_ see that segments are being forceMerged in the _tasks API now, but there’s no way to track the index they came from, which client process initiated them, or anything else that the _tasks API normally allows you to do.  I have been very hopeful that the _tasks API would allow me to do overcome these shortcomings, but in its current state, it’s no more than a gamble that even using the _tasks API would work as desired, since it would require (at the very least):

1. Dumping all tasks from all nodes
1. Combing through them to find forceMerges that may or may not have been initiated by me
1. Keep doing 1 and 2 until there are no more forceMerges 

The huge problem is that if I query the _tasks API and there has been a slight pause in between new tasks, such that I see no forceMerges, but a few (or several) seconds later, the next shard in the index starts a forceMerge (this is a very real possibility, looking at the monitoring data. The replica shards are sometimes the last to do the forceMerge, and can have delays in between), Curator may mistakenly think that the forceMerge is done when it is not.  It would be no problem, if a real parent task were created, complete with a task ID. But a task ID is not generated by Elasticsearch for a forceMerge, which makes this a guess and a gamble.

Since you’re already using a Lambda, and you will be using the API for this, rather than Curator directly, your best bet will be to code around this as best you can by combining the tasks API with the expected `max_num_segments` and hope that it’s all done.  It is a lot of extra coding I wish you didn’t have to do, but it’s the only hope anyone currently has to overcome the API shortcomings I outlined, and those are still a guess and a gamble.  There are just no concrete solutions yet.

This feature is as simple as adding the [`opened` filtertype](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/client/curator/current/filtertype_opened.html) :

```
- filtertype: opened
  exclude: True
```

This will exclude all open indices.  You can even test this at the command line with the `curator_cli` command, like this:

```
$ curator_cli show_indices --filter_list '{"filtertype":"opened", "exclude":true}'
logstash-2017.03.01
metricbeat-2017.03.01
```
Note that this command only runs without extra flags if you have a properly configured `curator.yml` file in the executing user's home directory in a `.curator` subdirectory, i.e. `$HOME/.curator/curator.yml`.

Since this feature is already available, I'm closing this issue.

The reason you observe this behavior is because `--no-push` isn't a pure flag--you have it registered as `three_state_flag` which means it could have a value (true/false).  Thus, it is parsing `.` against the allowed values of `true/false`.

This is standard argparse behavior so we would not make alterations to the CLI to change this; however, it looks like `--no-push` is a behavioral flag on the command, which means pure flag behavior (`action='store_true'`) would be more appropriate than three state flag, which is really meant for boolean properties on resources.  If that's the case, you can change it to a pure flag and you wouldn't see this issue.

@asottile wrote:
> If we didn't support windows I'd probably use something like libmagic

In the shell-scripted pre-commit hooks I used before switching to this package, I used `file` magic to select files - this was especially useful for running checks against scripts where there was no file extension, but a simple hashbang check could tell that a particular file was Python, or Perl, or *sh...
I would love it if there were some simple anchored PCRE filter that could select files based on content (of, usually, the first line), so that I could do something like this in `hooks.yaml`:
```
-   id: autopep8-wrapper
    name: autopep8 wrapper
    description: "Runs autopep8 over python source..."
    entry: autopep8-wrapper
    language: python
    files: \.py$
    file-content: #! *?/.*?\bpython\d?\b
    args: [-i]
```
with the hook run against files that match either the filename check or the content check (anchored to start of file content, with `.` not matching newline). This particular regex would match `#! /usr/bin/python` or `#!/usr/bin/env python3.2` or various other kinds of combinations of these sorts of hashbang lines. (The optional space after the bang is allowed - see http://stackoverflow.com/questions/10197690).

I'm with @superbobry on this one--in my experience with the framework, it's possible to (implicitly) have non-fully-connected HMMs. I've got some data I'm working with which is inherently left-to-right, and whether or not I initialize with a transition matrix which forces left-to-right topology (i.e. eye(n_components)), I'll almost always end up with a transition matrix which reflects this desired structure.

However, as @matthiasplappert noted, you won't get 0 transition probabilities, just extremely low values. In practice, it strikes me that this is just fine--when you have transition probabilities which are more than 10 orders of magnitude below other entries, those transitions never occur in practice.

I would make an argument that, as provided, this module supports non-fully-connected HMMs.

@rgbkrk good call. I've switched it so that it only logs the token if it's been automatically generated. Setting a token via config would result in nothing logged.

> why does the notebook lack this functionality

Partly because unlimited undoing is a much more complex subject when the document is executable.
Which is the case with the notebook.

Example : if i do `a=1`, execute, `a=2`, execute, and undo what am I suppose to undo ? The execution ? 

Then you enter question like : Can the execution be undone ? Can it be undone reliably across languages ? What if other code is currently being ran ?.... etc, and I just scratch the surface. 

Other part : we assessed the benefit/cost ratio, and decided that with the current manpower/usage/cost/number-of-features-needed/current-architecture it was not worth implementing, at least yet.

This can of course change if there is a change in any of the above mentioned factor, especially if we get more help for code maintenance.

I'm closing as the multiple undo has already a few issues where it is discussed, but feel free to continue adding comments to this issues if you like. 

Thanks.

This is an issue some people do encounter, and there are two levels to it:

1. the cost of sending the text to the browser
2. the cost of putting the text on the page and adding it to the document

(1) can be prevented only by adding a prompt at a low level in the kernel (in sys.stdout.write or flush), whereas (2) can be done in js on the client-side. It won't protect the most egregious cases, but it is often the javascript that's the biggest problem, and not the network traffic.

Computation of `c1a` is meant to correct for the variance loss in `C` when `hsig` is zero. This should rarely matter, but IIRC I have seen one case where this made the difference between successful vs premature convergence. 

As long as the step-size is too small, any change of the covariance matrix is arguably unjustified because it comes from a "wrong" environment. Using repeatedly the "frozen" `pc` to update `C` may not at all be a good idea. It is probably comparatively easy to verify this empirically.

The 1st case of 

> Referencing a .NET Standard 1.4 or 1.6 class library from a .NET Core 1.1 Web App

is a duplicate of https://github.com/dotnet/coreclr/issues/10037.  In short, the scenario wasn't supported in the 1.1 time frame.  It only worked in 1.0 because of a bug in the dotnet host.  We only supported Project-to-Project references and NuGet references.

The 2nd case of 

> a .NET Standard 2.0 class library from a .NET Core 2.0 Web App fails at runtime.

Is tracked by https://github.com/dotnet/core-setup/issues/2981.  Basic console and WebAPI apps work in this scenario.  It is only ASP.NET MVC apps that are broken.  The fix has been merged into the `master` branch, and @steveharter is working on porting the change into a `2.0.x` service release.

Hi @Miserlou,

Why don't you or Amazon Lambda serve a redirect from port 80 to 443?  Then HTTP-01 will work for your use case.  That will also prevent people who naively type "your.example.com" into the address bars of older browsers will from getting a broken result (new browsers support HSTS preloading, but you need to make sure you've done that).

Turning of port 80 does not improve security, because it doesn't prevent network attackers from spoofing a port 80 for your service while they impersonate you.

We'd be open to an appropriate revision to ACME that adds an HTTP-02 that supports TLS but is safe from default vhost attacks.  It'll just take much longer for something like that to be specified and deployed.

I believe the actual cause of this is that at some point between 0.6 and 1.2, support for a path in the URL was broken.  When configured with a URL of `http://localhost:8938/es1.3/`, pyelasticsearch will try to access `http://localhost:8938/<indexname>/...` instead of `http://localhost:8938/es1.3/<indexname>/...`.

That matches the URL path in OP's error: `WARNING:elasticsearch:GET /_search?q= `.

I think the ideal state here is:

* No output is directly sent to `stdout`, with the exception of APIs like `print_rows` that explicitly print to the console. Such APIs should have non-print equivalents (like `rows`) that simply return the value as a string.
* No output is directly sent to `stderr`.
* Debug/info/warning/error messages are routed through the Python [logging](https://docs.python.org/2/library/logging.html) API, with a consistent and predictable module naming scheme (perhaps all of `turicreate`, or perhaps sub-modules), so the user can control from Python which strings are sent where.

Hi Thomas, it looks all good, I'd like to know why you've chosen to split the code the way you did.
For example; would it be a good idea to merge:
- Config.read_default_config / Config.read
- Config._set_default / Config.__init__
- Config._raise_validation_errors / Config.read_default_config
- Config.all_config_files / Config.system_config_files / Config.additional_files
- Config.read / Config.all_config_files
- Config.read / Config.read_config_files

This would certainly improve readability, but if you already know all of these functions will directly used from mycli/pgcli/mssql-cli we should keep them (of course) seperate.

@meeuw Thanks for the helpful feedback! I appreciate the good questions.

I personally prefer a maximum of two-to-three levels deep of conditional/loop indentation in functions, with large blocks of code within loops being split into functions, which is why some of that is broken out the way it is. I feel like it's easier to read indented code blocks when they are put in named functions.

I think some of the ones you mentioned make sense to merge. I'll try to rework these a bit so it's not as spread out:
- `_set_default` merged into `__init__`
- `_raise_validation_errors` into `read_default_config`

The other short file-based ones (`all_config_files`, `system_config_files`, `user_config_file`, and `additional_files`) make it very easy for client applications to override one or two pieces of the default configuration setup without affecting the rest. That might be my day-to-day Ruby programming shining through a bit.

Ultimately, my goal in this API was two-fold:
1. Make the default interface extremely easy-to-use (create a config object, then call `read()`).
2. Don't assume all client applications handle configuration the same way, so make it easy for them to customize various parts of the configuration process.

So, for number 1, we end up with one client-facing method that is the primary way to load the configuration -- `read()`.

For number 2, I tried to make it so client applications wouldn't have to replicate a whole lot of the logic if they want to override different portions of the config reading/writing:
- Which system config files to read
- Which user config file to read
- Which additional files to read (also controllable via the constructor)
- The reading of the files themselves
- The reading of the default config file (which might need different logic than the user-facing config files)
- The writing of the default config file
- The writing of the current, in-memory config to a file

- https://github.com/PyFilesystem/pyfilesystem2/blob/b8cc82be593b7c9dd203c96a08d99053668ed844/fs/osfs.py#L455

> Pickling doesn't make sense for many filesystems.

However, I don't think it is the business of the base class to, in effect, prevent pickling of all derived classes. It should be up to specific implementations to decide what they want to do.

> Should network filesystems store authentication?

[s3fs](http://s3fs.readthedocs.io/) is totally fine with pickling, and has well-defined behaviour for authentication. 

> What happens if you unpickle an OSFS on another machine

This is a real use case, you may share an NFS, for example. The unpickler could check for the existence of the path, but, again, this is up to the implementation.

When you create the new BVS to use as the replacement, claripy appends a unique identifier to it, making it a different BVS. There are two ways to avoid this:

1. Use the `explicit_name=True` keyword argument when creating the BVS: ```replacements[claripy.BVS('mem_ffffffa4_11_32', 32, explicit_name=True).cache_key] = claripy.BVS('mem', 32)```

2. Use the actual original AST:
```replacements[original_ast] = claripy.BVS('mem', 32)```

@adybbroe I think I've figured this out and it brought out another issue. It may be something that @mraspaud need to discuss further.

What is happening is that when you load the composite it fills a "dependency tree" graph with all of the prerequisites that will be required to make that composite. When you load `10.8`, instead of regenerating all of the dependency tree it first checks if it knows how to resolve `10.8` already. The first issue you're experiencing is that it is taking on "trunk" nodes of the dependency tree and trying to regenerate them. The second issue I've found is that if you were to load `3.7`, the Scene would find the modified version of `3.7` (nir_reflectance) before finding the correct dataset from the reader. You haven't encountered this issue directly yet, but it's still an issue.

For example the dependency tree (just names, not data) looks like this:

    - day_microphysics
        - 0.85
        - 3.7 (nir_reflectance)
            - 3.7
            - 11
            - solar_zenith_angle (optional)
            - 13.4 (optional)
        - 10.8
        
This dependency tree is created when you first load `day_microphysics`. The Scene will attempt to load all of the datasets and then generate the composite. After the composite is generated all of the dependencies are unloaded and only the composite Dataset remains, but the dependency tree still exists.

When you then ask for 10.8 it gathers all "trunk" nodes and decides tries to compute them if they are not in the loaded datasets. So that means it tries to find `3.7 (nir_reflectance)` and `day_microphysics`. The `day_microphysics` composite already exists so it doesn't need to load that. However, the `3.7 (nir_reflectance)` does get computed and that's why you get the warnings about missing prereqs. I think I have a fix for this in the same way I fixed the multi-loading from before.

The issue of asking for `3.7` but getting `3.7 (nir_reflectance)` is something @mraspaud and I will have to discuss. It may be a simple fix, it may not be. I'll let you know when I get these fixed.

I'll make some minor changes myself after merge - mainly, if a parameter is required, don't use self.get("required_param") since that will create a default value of None if unset, which won't cause the code to immediately fail and the failure will occur somewhere else in the code. Just use self["required_parameter"] so the code will immediately fail right then and there if the required parameter is not set.

Basically, the job of the code is not to avoid failures, but rather to ensure the code clearly tells the user where his/her mistakes are (not have the code fail 10 lines downstream of their mistake).

The original pager code was written to achieve specific results especially when paging data that wraps. If a user has a pager environment variable, there is a risk that paged data will be presented in a way that the developer did not intend and may even be unreadable. A user may then assume a problem exists with the cmd2 application.

This PR forces developers that want the default behavior to write custom code to enforce it in all cases. Therefore, I would rather we preserve the original code. If a developer wants different behavior, they are free to override the pager.

@alsast You may be looking for a tool which is fundamentally philosophically different than what ``cmd2`` is trying to be.  We are very much trying to be an extension of ``cmd`` which is based on  **readline** and we are inspired by the conventions and idioms commonly found in the **Bash** shell. 
 The vast majority of our users love this and are ideologically predisposed to this viewpoint.  We provide them with the ability to very quickly and easily create an extremely feature-rich interactive command-line application which follows the conventions and idioms they are already familiar with from other CLI tools on POSIX OSes.

That being said, there are a few things which may get you what you want.  First off, if you aren't already using it, ``cmd2`` now supports the ability to have a persistent **readline** history across runs of your program.  This makes **Ctrl-r** much more powerful.

Secondly, ``cmd2`` supports the ability to replace the completion key with something other than <Tab>.  See the [documentation](https://cmd2.readthedocs.io/en/latest/freefeatures.html?highlight=completekey#searchable-command-history) for the arguments passed to ``cmd.Cmd.__init__()``.

I think there may be a way to tell **readline** to replace the <Ctrl-r> keyboard shortcut for doing a reverse search with something else.  @kmvanbrunt is our **readline** expert, maybe he can tell you exactly what this is.

I would recommend using **pip** to install ``cmd2`` instead of using **apt-get** because you will get a newer version of ``cmd2`` that way, though either one should work fine.

I did the following on my Kali VM:
```Bash
apt-get update
apt-get upgrade
apt-get dist-upgrade
pip install -U cmd2
mkdir src
cd src
git clone https://github.com/python-cmd2/cmd2
cd cmd2
./cmd2.py
```
This updated all files on the Kali distro, installed ``cmd2`` using **pip**, cloned the ``cmd2`` git repo, and then ran a completely bare-bones vanilla ``cmd2`` application.  This all worked fine. 

P.S.  Kali *is* Debian-based.  If **apt-get** exists, you can pretty much safely assume it is a Debian-based Linux distribution.

> I used k_neighbors=1. Why do the algorithms says: Expected n_neighbors <= n_samples, but n_samples = 1, n_neighbors = 2?

This error is raised by the nearest-neighbours of scikit-learn where the parameter is then called n_neighbors and not k_neighbors or m_neighbors which are specific to SMOTE.

We could actually cash the error and raise a more appropriate error with the specific naming. PR welcomed.

By recalling the documentation:

* `k_neighbors` is number of nearest neighbours to used to construct synthetic samples.
* `n_neighbors` is the number of nearest neighbours to use to determine if a minority sample is in danger.

Therefore, if the nearest-neighbors is given a single sample at fit and has to use the 5 nearest-neighbors (which are not there since we have a single sample), it leads to the given error.

I'm not going to have much time until after PyCon, so I'll just give you a quick sketch:

1. Keep track of a monotonically increasing version number of OS deps for each machine. Write DB-style "migrations" to move from each version to the next, installing whatever new deps are necessary. Hi @lautiamkok !
So that note in the Arch Linux guide refers specifically to the webroot challenge.
For certbot to get you a certificate from Let's Encrypt it can perform any one of three challenges. Once you have that certificate you can use whatever you like to serve it (such as Shiny Server or a Node JS Server).
If you don't have a webserver occupying port 80 or 443 on your server you might try the `standalone` command. If you have something like apache running in front of your shiny server you could use the `apache` command, or if you are hosting things on port 80, the `webroot` command might actually be right for you.
You can read more about these commands here: https://certbot.eff.org/docs/using.html#certbot-commands
Once you use one of these to obtain a certificate you'll have to figure out how Shiny or Node would like you to serve them. Feel free to let me know how it goes here, or you're also welcome to come chat in #letsencrypt on irc.freenode.net if you want more interactive/hands on help!

configuration should be stored in simple text file and user should edit it rarely. (like `/etc/` or dotfiles in UNIX).
state is what sqlite in raiden is now and it should be maintained by the app. (`/var`, `/srv` & co. )

One by one, let me first streamline the generation and execution of tasks, basically sending tasks to workers right after they are created.

We had something like this when all parameters were defined in a `[parameters]` section. It is more difficult to do now because parameters are scattered in the script, and are parsed only when the steps are called. In the extreme case, parameters are only invoked with dynamically created subworkflows. That is to say, we cannot determine in advance which parameters belong to which workflow, etc.

So the best that can be done, but perhaps already good enough, is to list all parameter definitions (and their comments) in the script, regardless the workflow. Then there can be weird things such as parameters with the same  names but different type/meaning that were designed for different workflows.

Also, the csv might contain several columns, and you might only be interested in a subset of those.While possible to write a somewhat generic dataset, the interface might get clumsy, and one might get tempted to extend it to handle specific use-cases, making something which was supposed to be easy complicated.

To close this issue, I'll post a snippet of how one can go to writing their own dataset for csv-like files:

```python
import pandas as pd

class PandasDataset(object):
    def __init__(self, path_to_csv_file, input_name, target_name):
        self.dataset = pd.read_csv(path_to_csv_file)
        self.input_name = input_name
        self.target_name = target_name
        # add transforms as well

    def __getitem__(self, idx):
        item = self.dataset.iloc[idx]
        # add transforms
        return item[self.input_name], item[self.target_name]

    def __len__(self):
        return len(self.dataset)
```

there are two common use cases:
- fully dense outputs
- adaptively pooled outputs

fully dense is the case where we give bigger inputs and we get bigger outputs than 1000x1x1

adaptive is when we give bigger inputs and always get out 1000x4096 to be fed into fc layers.

they are both common and in conflicting paths.

we should plan for these both via constructor arguments

`alt` is alternative. It's a stream of the same type (`hls`/`hds`/etc.) and the same quality. So if there are two`720p hls` streams then one will be named `720p` and the other `720p_alt`. `ds = DataFlowProducesIndices()` will only exist in one process. It will not be forked.
`MultiProcessMapData` creates processes to only run the mapper function.

After some investigation and communication on gitter, it turns out that it is an expected behavior that not all files are cached.
Since each worker created by `PrefetchDataZMQ` randomly produces images independently, when the number of workers is larger than one, there will be duplication and therefore not all files are read.
To ensure a whole dataset is used, use indices + `MultiProcessMapData` (https://github.com/tensorpack/tensorpack/pull/794#issuecomment-398429188)

Some more details:
Assuming there are K workers, each worker will create a local shuffle and produce roughly the first N/K images in the first epoch. The probability for a particular worker to produce a particular image is 1/K.
The probability an image will not appear in the first epoch will be `[(K-1)/K]^K`.
When K=40, this number is 0.36 so the cache will be 64% full. Considering the prefetching the cache should be a little bit more than 64% full.

Thanks - you've done some great work here. There is one more subtle race condition. Because the output capturing happens in the browser, and works with a stack of capturers, things could get messed up if you have two threads both producing output. For example, if this is the order of events:

Thread 1 creates an output widget and starts the output context
Thread 1 prints "T1:A"
Thread 2 creates an output widget and starts the output context
Thread 2 prints "T2:A"
Thread 1 prints "T1:B"

Then thread 2's output capturing will get "T1:B".

The reliable way to do things here is to not use output capturing, but to directly append values to the output widgets, with `out.append_stdout`, `out.append_stderr`, or `out.display_data` (these were added in https://github.com/jupyter-widgets/ipywidgets/pull/1752, released in ipywidgets 7.0.3).

@tgoodlet ah, yeah, what you're hitting is a different frustrating problem. The problem is that neither you nor Python are taking responsibility for making sure your async generator object gets cleaned up properly. There's more discussion of this in [PEP 533](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0533/) (which is stalled, unfortunately). For now the only real workaround is to replace your `async for` with a clunky `async with`+`async for` combination:

```python
from async_generator import aclosing

async def iterate_nums():
    ...
    async with aclosing(nums(10)) as ait:
        async for i in ait:
            ...
```

See also:

* [Some docs](https://async-generator.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference.html#context-managers)

* #265 discusses whether there's anything trio can do to mitigate this issue (though unfortunately we can't really fix it properly – in particular nurseries inside async generators absolutely require something like the `aclosing` hack).

> This is like overloading by return types in C/C++ (which is an impossibility) and should be avoided.

ONNX is not C++, and thus different design constraints apply. In the case of ONNX, every operator always has an explicit list of outputs, so there is no problem with overloads. The analogous situation in C++ would be if you never "returned" from a function, but instead passed in references for outputs (e.g., `f(int in1, int in2, int& out1, int& out2)`); in this case, it is clear that overloading can be made to work. The TF split situation is different, because here split is a Python function to generate a node in question.

Now, the internal representation doesn't necessarily correspond one-to-one with how you should implement a DSL for defining ONNX nodes. In the case of PyTorch, we have an `outputs` parameter which specifies how many outputs you want to create an ONNX node with.

Thank you for the reports, @hvpham. I calculated squared errors between different backends' outputs with one single image:

|                   | TF & TH | TF & C |
|-------------------|---------|--------|
| VGG16             | 5e-16   | 3e-17  |
| DenseNet121       | 4e-16   | 5e-8   |
| MobileNet         | 1e-15   | 1e-8   |
| InceptionV3       | 1e-15   | 1e-7   |
| InceptionResNetV2 | 1e-15   | 1e-4   |

When a squared error of single softmax output is about 1e-8, a difference of performances on ImageNet validation set is usually less than 1%. But, InceptionResNetV2 with CNTK obviously produced different results as you told.

In results, they all came from the disagreement of BatchNormalization:
- TF/TH: `(x - mean) / C.sqrt(var + epsilon) * gamma + beta`,
- C: `(x - mean) / (C.sqrt(var) + epsilon) * gamma + beta`.

Due to the disagreement, the more BatchNormalization is done, the more the differences accumulate. But the disagreement has been resolved now (keras-team/keras#10427). **Thus, you can get the same outputs among all the backends if you use the bleeding edge of Keras.**

Well, the problem is that once console_skript is invoked via paver command, your syslib's paver is used onwards, leading to further import fun.

It can be softoffixed by playing with sys.path and reload()ing paver before invoking nosetests, but it still leads to some funny consequences when some parts are seeing some versions from some paver modules. "Solution" would probably be to inspect and reload everything, but that seems rather hacky.

Rather than hacking around, I'd say I'll mention above suggestions in docs and use them.

Update: My mistake, calling it without global_ns in 0.11 will take the global namespace of the module it's called from, which probably isn't what you want. This might require an extra try/except to decide whether to pass an empty global_ns dict or a new `types.ModuleType()` object.
